data.title: Appeals court won't reconsider Habba disqualification, paving way to Supreme Court
data.description:
A federal appeals court has declined to reconsider a three-judge panel’s decision to uphold Alina Habba’s disqualification as the top federal prosecutor in New Jersey, paving the way for the Justice Department to take President Trump’s fight to retain loyalist U.S. attorneys to the Supreme Court.

In a terse order Monday, the U.S. Court of Appeals for the 3rd Circuit said the judges who ruled that Habba, a former personal attorney to Trump, was unlawfully appointed as U.S. attorney for New Jersey did not ask to rehear the case. A majority of judges voted against putting the matter in front of the full court, as well.

Three of the court’s 11 en banc judges would have granted the Justice Department’s request to rehear the case, and one of them will publish a dissent “on a later date,” the order said.

Judge Emil Bove, another of Trump’s former attorneys who was appointed by the president to the bench, did not take part in the vote.

The decision keeps intact the panel’s ruling that Habba’s tenure became unlawful when she remained in the role after her 120-day interim term expired, despite the “novel series of legal and personnel moves” the administration took to keep her in the job.  

The ruling keeps Habba away from the helm of New Jersey’s federal prosecuting office, for now, but it may also clear the way for a Supreme Court battle over Trump’s efforts to keep his preferred prosecutors in U.S. attorney offices across the country.

Habba resigned from her role last month, following the panel’s ruling, but she wrote in a sworn declaration that she intends to return to lead the office if a higher court rules in her favor.

The Justice Department told the 3rd Circuit judges earlier this month the panel’s “atextual limits” on acting U.S. attorneys is a matter of “exceptional importance.” 

When Habba’s interim term ended in July, New Jersey federal judges declined to extend her temporary tenure and instead invoked a seldom-used power to appoint her first assistant U.S. attorney to the position.  

However, so Habba could remain atop the office, Attorney General Pam Bondi fired the judges’ selected successor and Trump withdrew Habba’s formal nomination to the role. She was given the title of acting U.S. attorney and the powers of the top prosecutor.

Judge D. Michael Fisher, an appointee of former President George W. Bush, wrote for the unanimous panel it is “apparent that the current administration has been frustrated by some of the legal and political barriers to getting its appointees in place.”

However, the panel agreed the maneuver runs afoul of the plain text of the law that establishes requirements for temporarily filling vacant U.S. attorney positions. 

“There is a time before ‘the President submits a nomination,’ and there is a time after ‘the President submits a nomination,’” Fisher wrote. “Just because the President later may withdraw the nomination does not erase the fact that he submitted it.” 

Habba was the first of Trump’s loyalist U.S. attorneys to be disqualified, but four others have since faced similar fates. They represent the California district that covers Los Angeles, Nevada, the Northern District of New York and the Eastern District of Virginia.

After Habba stepped down and three prosecutors split up her duties, Delaware’s GOP chair-turned-top federal prosecutor announced her resignation, as well, citing the appeals court’s decision.

The Virginia prosecutor, Lindsey Halligan, is reportedly no longer with the Justice Department after resigning last week. A judge barred her from “representing herself as the United States Attorney in any pleading or otherwise before this Court until such time as she may lawfully hold the office either by Senate confirmation or appointment by this Court … should either occur.”

Halligan’s disqualification led to dismissals of the federal criminal cases against former FBI Director James Comey and New York Attorney General Letitia James (D), two of Trump’s foremost foes.

The Hill requested comment from the Justice Department.

data.title: Treasury cancels Booz Allen contracts
data.description:
The Treasury Department on Monday said it would cancel 31 contracts with Booz Allen Hamilton, citing its failure to ” implement adequate safeguards” after a past employee leaked tax data, including President Trump’s returns, to media outlets.

The decision to cut ties with the company was due in part to the misconduct of Charles Edward Littlejohn, who stole and leaked the confidential tax returns of some of the country’s wealthiest individuals, ultimately impacting 406,000 taxpayers, Treasury said in a release announcing the cancellations.

Littlejohn, is a former IRS contractor who provided data to The New York Times and ProPublica between 2018 and 2020, after figuring out how to extract tax data without raising internal flags. He was sentenced to five years in prison after pleading guilty to leaking tax information.

“Booz Allen failed to implement adequate safeguards to protect sensitive data, including the confidential taxpayer information it had access to through its contracts with the Internal Revenue Service,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in the statement.

Littlejohn disclosed more than a decade of tax returns for thousands, including Trump, whom the outlets said paid $750 in federal income taxes in 2016 to 2017.

In total, the Booz Allen contracts were valued in $4.8 million in annual spending and $21 million in obligations, according to the Treasury.

The Treasury’s move follows the Pentagon’s decision to discontinue a Defense Health Agency contract for consulting services from Accenture, Deloitte, Booz Allen and other firms.

Still, the company said it was “surprised” by the Treasury’s announcement to cancel all of its contracts.

“Booz Allen has zero tolerance for violations of the law and operates under the highest ethical and professional guidelines. When Littlejohn’s criminal conduct occurred more than five years ago, it was on government systems, not Booz Allen systems. Booz Allen stores no taxpayer data on its systems and has no ability to monitor activity on government networks,” a company spokesperson told The Hill.

“Booz Allen fully supported the U.S. government in its investigation, and the government expressed gratitude for our assistance, which led to Littlejohn’s prosecution. We were surprised by this announcement and look forward to discussing this matter with Treasury,” they added.

data.title: Pence: Images of Pretti shooting 'deeply troubling'
data.description:
Former Vice President Mike Pence on Monday characterized the footage of a Border Patrol agent shooting Minneapolis resident Alex Pretti as “deeply troubling,” as he called for a thorough investigation into the incident.  

“In the wake of the tragic shooting that claimed the life of Alex Pretti this weekend, our prayers are with his family, the citizens of Minneapolis and local, state and federal law enforcement officers serving there,” Pence said in a post on the social platform X. 

“The images of this incident are deeply troubling and a full and transparent investigation of this officer involved shooting must take place immediately,” he continued.  

The former vice president also urged law enforcement from different levels of government to work together, amid concerns that federal authorities are blocking state officials from participating in the investigation.  

“The focus now should be to bring together law enforcement at every level to address the concerns in the community even while ensuring that dangerous illegal aliens are apprehended and no longer a threat to families in Minneapolis,” Pence said.  

The fatal shooting of 37-year-old Pretti, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis Veterans Affairs hospital, has escalated tensions in the city, where demonstrations against the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown have continued since a federal officer shot and killed Renee Good, another 37-year-old Minneapolis resident, earlier this month.    

The Trump administration has defended the Pretti shooting, saying the agent fired “defensive shots” at Pretti, who had a gun that was recovered by federal authorities. Others have pointed to video footage that appears to contradict that version of events, accusing the agent of acting with excessive force as Pretti held his phone to film Immigration and Customs Enforcement officers before the incident escalated.   

Pence said it’s important for Americans’ First Amendment rights co-exist with law and order.  

“The American people deserve to have safe streets, our laws enforced and our constitutional rights of Freedom of Speech, peaceable assembly and the right to keep and bear Arms respected and preserved all at the same time. That’s how Law and Order and Freedom work together in America,” he added in his post. 

The incident, and the Trump administration’s response to it, has garnered some pushback among even some Republicans, who have called for an investigation into the shooting and have expressed support for free speech and peaceful protest. 

Pence has frequently found himself at odds with President Trump since the 2020 election, when he refused to comply with Trump’s efforts to overturn the election results in his favor.

data.title: Tariffs reward inefficiency. Here's a better way.
data.description:
Tariffs have returned to the center of the U.S. economic debate, as their growing use as a political tool coincides with an impending Supreme Court decision. For more than 200 years, the U.S. has repeatedly transformed its economy while benefiting from global trade. Lately, however, we have turned to tariffs, an inefficient means of supporting high-cost production processes.

These policies can offer short-term political gains, but they do so at the expense of long-term competitiveness. There is a better approach: limited, sunsetting price subsidies for small and medium-sized businesses, tied to new capital investment, to help modernize the country’s industrial base. Unlike tariffs, which reward inefficiency and lock it in place, this approach preserves competitive pressure while forcing firms to earn their survival through productivity gains.

America’s economic story is one of reinvention. In 1800, 5 million people generated roughly $500 million in economic activity; today, 350 million people produce about $30 trillion. In real terms, GDP per capita has increased approximately 45 times — from roughly $2,000 to $90,000 — when measured in 2025 dollars. This growth did not occur by accident. It reflects businesses continually upgrading technology and reorganizing production as they compete at home and abroad, with more and higher-paying jobs replacing those lost to progress. Shielding industries through tariffs slows this process of job creation and productivity growth.

Innovation has repeatedly transformed farms, factories and services. Mechanical inventions reshaped agriculture and manufacturing; process improvements, such as the assembly line and just-in-time systems, boosted scale and efficiency; semiconductors launched the digital era; and now AI is transforming the economy again. Each wave of innovation built on prior productivity gains, demonstrating the compound benefits of ongoing technological adoption.

Trade has long reinforced this progress. Some goods are best produced elsewhere because of natural resources and geographic constraints — coffee and bananas are obvious examples. But tariffs do the opposite: They lock in high-cost domestic production and operate as a tax on imported intermediate and final goods — raising costs for domestic producers and consumers throughout the supply chain.

Tariffs are also a blunt tool for confronting state-backed competitors willing to absorb steep economic losses to dominate strategic industries such as rare earths and pharmaceuticals. And there is an irony at the heart of these policies. When tariffs generate large sums of revenue — as supporters often claim — that is usually a sign that imports are still coming in and domestic production has not expanded enough to replace them. In that sense, tariff revenue is not evidence of success. More often, it reflects how stubbornly the underlying problem persists. Worse still, while tariffs lock in high domestic costs, they simultaneously incentivize exporting countries to reduce their own costs.

A better path is to promote policies that return the U.S. to innovative growth — not only for the trillion-dollar firms leading in AI, but also for the millions of small and medium-sized businesses that employ nearly half of the American workforce. The goal should be transition, not protection: maintain competitive pressure while giving firms time and support to modernize.

The country should adopt a transitional, rules-based program of temporary price subsidies that is industry-neutral and designed to accelerate modernization. Under this proposal, the government would cover 90 percent of the import-price gap for a qualifying company for three years, followed by 75 percent coverage for the next three years, using Department of Commerce price data. The subsidy would end after six years.

The program would be limited to small and medium-sized businesses with up to $1 billion in annual revenue. It is designed to help viable firms modernize, not to keep failing ones afloat. To qualify, a company’s domestic prices could be no more than 30 percent higher than import prices, which is slightly above the current average tariff rate. Subsidies would be capped at the amount needed to eliminate an operating loss, ensuring there is no profit guarantee. To stay in the program, companies would also have to reinvest at least half of the first year’s support into new equipment or other capital investments. By tying assistance to real investment, the goal is lasting productivity gains, not short-term financial relief.

This approach would modernize the industrial base without targeting specific countries or relying on discretionary, sector-by-sector picking of winners and losers. To avoid creating yet another permanent program, it should be revenue-neutral, with offsets through spending cuts or tax increases.

The U.S. annually imports about $3 trillion in goods. As an order-of-magnitude estimate, suppose the larger small and medium-sized businesses could compete for $1 trillion in imports, and this program captures 25 percent of that market. At the maximum 30 percent price allowance, the first-year subsidy would be approximately $75 billion, supporting several thousand import-exposed small and mid-sized firms collectively employing over 1 million workers, while remaining constrained by price caps, loss limits and investment requirements.

There will still be situations where the government must confront bad actors more directly — through strategic reserves for critical materials and pharmaceuticals, long-term purchase contracts, and, in extreme cases, ending trade with specific foreign entities. But for the vast majority of international trade, the better answer is not tariffs that promise security but deliver stagnation. A temporary, rules-based transition program would put the country back on the path of competitive innovation — a road the U.S. has traveled for more than two centuries — and would offer a fiscally responsible alternative to tariffs that fosters durable growth across the economy.

Robert Goldberg is the James F. Bender Clinical Professor of Finance at Adelphi University on Long Island, N.Y.

data.title: Schumer: Democrats will speed up non-DHS funding bills ahead of shutdown deadline
data.description:
Senate Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer (N.Y.) announced Monday that Senate Democrats are prepared to speed up passage of five government funding bills — but not funding for the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) — to avoid a large swath of the government shutting down at the end of the week.

“Senate Democrats have made clear we are ready to quickly advance the five appropriations bills separately from the DHS funding bill before the January 30th deadline. The responsibility to prevent a partial government shutdown is on Leader Thune and Senate Republicans,” Schumer said in a statement, referring to Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.).

“If Leader Thune puts those five bills on the floor this week, we can pass them right away. If not, Republicans will again be responsible for another government shutdown,” Schumer said.

Thune has yet to say what his plan is for avoiding a partial government shutdown. Senate GOP aides expect him to advance a six-bill funding package passed by the House last week, but Democrats announced over the weekend that they would block it unless the Homeland Security appropriations bill is stripped out.

Both chambers have already passed six of the 12 funding bills. The House passed the remaining six in several batches alongside a rule that combined them into one package to send to the Senate.

Thune would need unanimous consent to strip the Homeland Security funding measure from the package but he could get 96 percent of government funding by passing the five other appropriations bills through the Senate this week.

The Senate still needs to pass bills funding the Departments of Defense, Labor, Health and Human Services, Education, Transportation, State along with operations related to financial services.

Typically it takes at least a week to pass a single appropriations bill on the Senate floor given the variety of procedural steps that are required. But Schumer says that all members of his caucus are willing to waive these obstacles to speed all five of the non-Homeland Security spending bills across the Senate floor.

data.title: Majorities see education, housing as unaffordable: Survey
data.description:
A new poll shows that more Americans view education and housing as unaffordable relative to other necessities.

The survey from The New York Times and the Siena Research Institute, released Monday, found that 58 percent of respondents view education as unaffordable, with 26 percent saying it is somewhat affordable and just 8 percent saying it is mostly affordable. 

A slightly lower share of respondents, 54 percent, said they cannot afford housing, with 31 percent saying it is somewhat affordable and 13 percent saying it is mostly affordable.

Education and housing were the two items viewed as unaffordable by the largest shares of respondents, with 47 percent saying health care is unaffordable, 44 percent saying having a family is unaffordable and fewer than 30 saying groceries, food, utilities and transportation individually are unaffordable.

In November, the Education Data Initiative reported that the average annual costs of tuition at 4-year public and private colleges was 40 and 35 times higher, respectively, than they were in 1963. 

The cost of shelter, meanwhile, increased 3.2 percent year-over-year in December, outpacing the overall inflation rate of 2.7 percent, according to the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

The Treasury Department said in 2024 that more than 90 percent of Americans lived in counties where median rents and house prices increased faster than median incomes from 2000 to 2020. The department cited housing demand outpacing housing supply this century. 

Earlier this month, President Trump signed an executive order directing his administration to crack down on Wall Street investors purchasing homes. However, houses bought by Wall Street investors accounted for roughly 3 percent of total home purchases in the third quarter of 2025, Redfin Chief Economist Daryl Fairweather told The Hill last week. 

The New York Times/Siena poll was conducted via interviews over the phone and by text with 1,625 registered voters from Jan. 12-17. It has a margin of error of 2.8 percentage points.

data.title: DHS says there's body cam footage of Pretti shooting
data.description:
The Department of Homeland Security (DHS) said multiple Customs and Border Protection officers were wearing body cameras during the fatal shooting of protester Alex Pretti on Saturday in Minneapolis. 

Camera footage from various angles is being reviewed by investigators, a DHS official told The Hill’s partner network NewsNation.

Outrage from lawmakers, former presidents and the public have bubbled after bystander footage showed Pretti being shot after recording with his phone. 

Greg Bongino, commander-at-large of the Border Patrol, accused Pretti of approaching officers with a handgun.

“The victims are the Border Patrol agents. I’m not blaming the Border Patrol agents,” Bovino said during a Sunday interview with CNN’s Dana Bash. 

“The victims are the Border Patrol agents. The suspect put himself in that situation,” he added.

President Trump in response to outrage sent border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota to provide a “fair” response to increased demonstrations.

“I am sending Tom Homan to Minnesota tonight. He has not been involved in that area, but knows and likes many of the people there,” Trump wrote Monday on Truth Social, adding that he had a good call with Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) earlier in the day. 

“Tom is tough but fair and will report to me,” Trump wrote.

data.title: Democrats can and should use a shutdown threat to abolish ICE
data.description:
As the Jan. 30 funding deadline approaches, Washington is bracing for a familiar ritual: the government shutdown standoff. But this time, the stakes are different, and the political math has fundamentally shifted. For Democratic leadership, the choice is no longer just about keeping the lights on. It’s recognizing a rare moment of alignment between moral necessity and hard-nosed pragmatism.

Democrats should not fear a shutdown. In fact, they should refuse to reopen the government until Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) is abolished. While such a stance was once labeled radical, the reality of 2026 suggests it is now the most practical exit strategy for both parties.

For years, the abolish ICE movement was treated as part of the political fringe. Today, it is a reflection of the median voter’s fatigue. Recent polling from The New York Times and Siena University paints a devastating picture for the agency: 61 percent of American voters now disapprove of ICE’s tactics, including 71 percent of Independents.  

The killings of Renee Good and Alex Pretti in Minneapolis this month, both captured on video and seen by most of the public, have served as a breaking point. When a majority of Americans, including a significant portion of Independents, view a federal agency’s tactics as unjustified and too forceful, that agency can no longer be an effective tool of public safety. 

While Republicans have historically defended ICE, the agency is becoming brand poison for the GOP. Under the weight of Operation Metro Surge, the visuals of militarized federal agents in American suburbs have alienated the very swing voters the Republican Party needs to win elections. 

For Republicans, ICE is now a political albatross. Every day a federal agent is filmed in a high-profile confrontation in a city like Minneapolis or Portland, Maine, the Republican “law and order” message is undermined by the reality of federal overreach. By forcing a shutdown over ICE funding, Democrats would be offering Republicans an off-ramp from a policy of blind support that is dragging down their approval ratings.

The practical argument for abolition isn’t about ending border security, it’s about a political reset on the issue. Abolishing ICE allows for the reallocation of its budget toward more effective, accountable and less controversial agencies.

Rep. Seth Moulton’s (D-Mass.) recent proposal to divert these funds toward lapsed Affordable Care Act tax credits is a prime example of a practical shift. It trades a high-conflict, low-approval agency for popular, tangible benefits that stabilize the economy. Folding essential ICE duties into the Department of Justice or traditional Customs and Border Protection enforcement roles would remove the rogue culture that has come to define the agency, replacing it with standard professional oversight.

Critics may point to the long odds of passing such a bill, given the number of votes needed and the likelihood of President Trump vetoing it. But doing so would put the president in an impossible spot. If he vetoes a bill that lowers health care premiums for millions just to protect an agency that only hardcore MAGA supports, he hands the initiative right back to his opponents. 

A veto isn’t a dead end, it’s a trap. It would force more Republican senators in moderate states to go on the record. They would have to explain to their constituents why they’re keeping the government shuttered and health care expensive just to defend a federal agency accused of being responsible for deaths of two Americans and is refusing local investigative cooperation. 

Forcing that veto-proof override vote is the point. I suspect many Republicans are privately seeking an exit strategy from the administration’s immigration enforcement tactics, but they need the cover of a governing crisis to finally distance themselves from the White House. By holding the line, Democrats provide that permission structure. They make it a choice between violence and chaos and practical, funded governance.

Democrats have the leverage of the Jan. 30 deadline. They should use it to demand a total freeze on ICE funding as a bridge to its eventual dissolution. If the government stays closed, the blame will fall on those fighting to protect an agency operating at an unrecoverable trust deficit, pushing out transparent propaganda that Republican politicians are struggling to coherently defend. 

In the high-stakes poker game of D.C. appropriations, you only bet big when you have the cards. With public opinion trending sharply towards a fundamental overhaul of immigration enforcement, Democrats aren’t just holding a winning hand, they’re holding the only hand that leads to a stable, long-term resolution — reassuring a weary public that the rule of law stands and that the separation of powers is once again intact. 

Logan McMillen works in library information services and studies the history of authoritarianism. His writing has previously appeared in The New Republic and The Minnesota Star Tribune.

data.title: Aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln in Middle East, expanding Trump military options against Iran
data.description:
The aircraft carrier USS Abraham Lincoln and its three accompanying warships have sailed into the Middle East region, providing President Trump additional military firepower if he decides to strike Iran. 

The USS Abraham Lincoln, which is equipped with Mk 57 Mod3 Sea Sparrow surface-to-air missile launchers and can carry squadrons of bombers and early warning planes, and its strike group are in the U.S. Central Command (Centcom) area, which encompasses the Middle East region, the command confirmed Monday.

The addition of the carrier in the region comes as Trump warned Thursday that the U.S. is sending an “armada” toward Iran following nationwide protests in the country, where thousands of demonstrators have been killed by authorities. 

“We have a big flotilla going in that direction,” the president told reporters aboard Air Force One. “We have a big force going toward Iran. I’d rather not see anything happen, but we’re watching them very closely.”

Over the weekend, the commander of Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, Gen. Mohammad Pakpour, said the nation has its “finger on the trigger” at the U.S. following Trump’s warning. 

“The Islamic Revolutionary Guard and dear Iran stand more ready than ever, finger on the trigger, to execute the orders and directives of the Commander-in-Chief,” Pakpour said on Saturday. 

Trump refrained from authorizing strikes on Iran as the demonstrations against the regime intensified earlier this month, and he said last week that he stopped Iran’s leadership from hanging 837 people.

The death toll is currently unclear as large swaths of the country are affected by the internet blackout, but a human rights group, HRANA, has reported over 5,800 confirmed deaths since the protests broke out in late December. 

The Lincoln, a Nimitz-Class nuclear-powered carrier, can house more than 5,000 personnel and carry EA-18G Growler electronic-warfare jets, F/A-18 jet planes and F-35Cs. Its Navy destroyers can fire long-range Tomahawk cruise missiles.

data.title: Major airline officially ends longstanding seat policy: 'Everything majorly changes'
data.description:
(NEXSTAR) – Travelers flying with Southwest Airlines are preparing to adjust to the airline’s new assigned-seating policy, which replaces the carrier’s longstanding open-seating arrangement as of Jan. 27.

“Southwest Airlines has no assigned seats for one more day,” one traveler flying to Kansas City remarked in an X post on Monday. “On my way back, everything majorly changes as I already have my assigned seat.”

What’s changing?

Southwest had first announced plans to ditch its longstanding open-seating policy in July 2024, in part to boost revenue. But the company also claimed its passengers increasingly preferred an assigned seating model with premium seating options.

“Our Customers want more choice and greater control over their travel experience,” Southwest executive Tony Roach said in 2025. “Assigned seating unlocks new opportunities for our Customers — including the ability to select Extra Legroom seats — and removes the uncertainty of not knowing where they will sit in the cabin.”

In the summer of 2025, Southwest also began booking assigned seats for passengers whose itineraries included flights after the Jan. 27 start date.

Some passengers appeared to welcome the change, saying it would cut down on “bad passenger behavior” and “abuses of open seating.” Others said it gave them a sense of certainty about the boarding process.

“I for one am SOOOOOOOOO happy to be rid of that open seating nonsense,” one X user wrote. “At 6’5″, I will GLADLY pay a premium for my favorite exit row seat with the leg room and not need to stand in line like cattle.”

Others, however, were less enthused. Critics argued that open-seating was preferable for guests who booked in groups, or passengers who had to change flights last-minute (as they wouldn’t be immediately relegated to the least-preferable seats on the plane, as might have otherwise happened on a different airline). Some felt the switch to assigned seating was just a money-making tactic.

“They had a good run but it’s obvious corporate greed won over what we all felt made them the best airline,” one social-media user claimed.

A spokesperson for Southwest was not immediately available to comment. But on social media, a representative for the carrier responded to a traveler who appeared upset by the changes.

“We’re evolving to meet changing Customer preferences, & our research clearly indicates a desire for assigned seating,” the representative wrote in an X response. “Still, we know assigned seating is not preferable for every Customer. We hope you’ll give us an opportunity to welcome you onboard.”

How does Southwest’s assigned seating work?

Customers booking flights with Southwest are now instructed to select from three seating options: “Standard” seats, located near the back of the cabin; “Preferred” seats, located nearer the front; and “Extra Legroom” seats, which are located near the exit rows and offer between 3 and 5 inches of extra legroom.

The type of seat a passenger purchases determines when they board, or whether they’re eligible for other perks, Southwest’s website explains. The changes also mean slight tweaks to Southwest’s pre-boarding policies for active-duty military members or families with children.

What other policies are changing?

In addition to its seating policy, Southwest on Tuesday is also changing its policy for “customers of size” who wish to use an extra seat.

Before Jan. 27, plus-size passengers were not required to purchase a second seat in advance, though it was recommended. (Additional seats for larger customers were provided free of charge “if space is available on the flight,” according to Southwest.) Refunds could be obtained after travel.

But beginning on Jan. 27, customers must buy an extra seat and “pay any applicable seat fee” before boarding. Refunds can still be obtained after travel, but eligibility will depend on a number of specific conditions, namely, that both seats were in the same fare class, the refund request was made within 90 days of travel, and the plane would have taken off with “at least one” extra open seat, according to Southwest.

Plus-size passengers can also be refunded if the plane was full, but the manifest included passengers who boarded with “space available passes” — e.g., passes given to airline employees or other non-paying passengers who are traveling for leisure.

After announcing the new policy earlier this year, advocates for plus-size travelers were disappointed by the policy change, with one group — the National Association to Advance Fat Acceptance — telling The New York Times that Southwest had been a “beacon of hope for many fat people who otherwise wouldn’t have been flying.”

Despite the upcoming changes to Southwest’s plus-size seating policy, the carrier is still the only major U.S. airline (out of American, Delta and United) that offers any type of refund for eligible plus-size passengers after the flight.

Earlier this year, Southwest said its agents were attempting to notify former fliers of the new policy in advance of their next trip with the airline.

“To ensure space, we are communicating to Customers who have previously used the extra seat policy they should purchase it at booking,” a spokesperson said.
